6th Annual Bog Slog, St Edward State Park - Sunday, December 29, 1985

This season's Bog Slog was notable for its good weather (no precip) and close times with few DQ's. Pie awards went to Steven & April Womack, Katie Sprugel, Dave Enger, Eileen Williams, Tony Paulson and Valerie Enger.

Page 6

O-meet Notes

The Kelsey Creek Park meet on Saturday, January 11th, provided a good example of a challenging course in a small park map on which many of the participants had run before. Kudos to course designer, Bob Rein, and his coach, Dave Enger, for a course where 1- the markers were placed at eye level and on the indicated side of the feature; and 2- the time advantage went to the cross-country route choice, which encouraged orienteers to forsake the trails. The scale of the Kelsey Creek map, 1 to 3,000, being smaller than most of our park maps, gave some runners (Debbie and Robert, to mention a couple of names) the opportunity to do some mapping of their own when they ran off the map in search of controls 7 and 8 on the advanced course. P.S. - thanks to the Sammamish Club for the six new 'O' signs donated to Cascade.

Coming Attractions:

Sunday, March 9th - Kelsey Creek Park, Bellevue
A "motala" course will be set for the intermediate and advanced orienteers. This will involve memory, line, and score orienteering - all on the same course! The park is located on SE 4th Street just east of 128th Ave SE.

Sunday, April 6th - Island Crest Park, Mercer Island
Only the second event on this new 1:7,500 scale 5-color map. If you didn't attend the last one due to the rain, don't miss this one! The area is more challenging than many of the other city parks. Located on Island Crest Way about two miles south of I-90.

Wednesday, May 14th, 5:30-7:00pm, Marymoor Park, Redmond
First evening meet of the year! Meet in the picnic area east of the mansion.

Mike Schuh will be hosting a party in his apartment (1706 NW 57th #3) on Saturday evening, March 15, starting at about 7:30pm. Besides his dynamic presence, the main attraction of the evening will be a slide show of his trip to Hong Kong, and the 1986 Asia-Pacific Orienteering Championships. Please RSVP (work 356-6241, home 783-3960) and bring some munchies or something to drink.

Mike Schuh will hold a short clinic on course setting on the last two Sundays in March (23rd and 30th). Both meetings will be at his apartment (1706 NW 57th #3) from 7:00 pm to 9:00pm and will cover such topics as the basic principles of course setting, differences between novice and advanced courses, how to keep the participants happy, mistakes to avoid, how to estimate winning times, the mechanics of course printing, how to use IOF control description symbols, how to tie markers on to trees so that they can be untied yet won't blow away, and anything else he thinks of between now and then. If you are interested in the clinic, please call Mike (work 356-6241, home 783-3960) and plan to attend. The clinic will be suitable for both those who have never set courses as well as those who have.

Page 7

"All of the fountains of the great abyss burst forth, and the floodgates of the sky were opened. For forty days and forty nights heavy rain poured down on the earth... Higher and higher above the earth rose the waters, until all the highest mountains everywhere were submerged..."
[Genesis 7:11-12,19]

I looked out into the rainy pre-dawn gloom. It was incredibly wet and getting wetter. "If I see a line of animals walking by in pairs, I'll cancel the meet."

Don Denbo, the course setter for the day's meet, looked up from the pile of control markers he was sorting and agreed that that would be a proper course of action, especially if a large boat were to pull up to the nearby beach and begin loading the animals.

We were preparing to set out the last couple dozen markers for Cascade OC's local meet of Saturday, January 18. It was the third straight day of heavy rain in the Seattle area, heavier than usual, and we were questioning our sanity. Before the day would end, the rainfall would come close to setting a local record for 24 hour precipitation total - 4.14 inches. Thursday evening's weather had included thunderstorms with winds over 70 knots, and now it was still pouring. On Friday morning I had wondered what we would do if the bad weather continue through Saturday. On Saturday morning, faced with finding out first hand, I also wondered if anyone would show up.

The meet was in Seattle's Carkeek Park, in the northwestern part of the city along the shore of Puget Sound. In local orienteering circles, the park is infamous for its mud and steep slopes. During the spring and summer, the forest is quite thick with stinging nettles and general foliage. Aside from the weather, winter is the best time to orienteer in most of our city park maps, at least as far as visibility is concerned. But, as might be expected, winter meets run the risk of inclement winter weather, heavy rains being the most common. The period of January 16-18, however, was exceptionally wet.

By 9:00 we had all of the control markers in place and I began to wonder if the rest of the meet staff would show up (our local meets begin at 10:00). One of the first cars to arrive, however, was a gentleman and his daughter out to try orienteering for their first time. A quick question confirmed my suspicion: like myself, they were natives of the Seattle area - a little bit of rain was not going to keep THEM indoors!

By 10:00 nearly all of my meet staff had arrived along with a surprisingly good turnout - a total of fifty-five individuals, plus the small handful of meet workers. Somehow we did not have enough control cards, and so everyone was obliged to punch along the edges of their maps. It turned out that this problem, and the corresponding complication of results processing, was the worst problem that we had all day. That and the fact that, for the first time in my experience, the map actually changed during the course of the event.

Now, stories of heavy rains and blizzards are plentiful in the growing folklore of American orienteering. There is the story of the [1984? 1982? - forget which year] Troll cup being buried under snow and subsequently rescheduled. Or the 1983 Missouri State Championships, where the rains turned "crossable streams" into "impassable rivers". But I have a story that might top even those legends.

Aware of the unfairness that rising stormwaters can introduce in an orienteering course, Don marked on a map corrections map and the master maps mandatory (and safe) crossing points. This worked well. But we began to get reports from some of the early finishers that not all of the 'crossing points'

Page 8

were still crossable. Clearly, Pipers Creek, which runs the length of the map, was rising. Some of the tributaries were also rising, as Debbie Newell found out when she approached the seventh control on the advanced course.

As she considered going up the side stream that lead to the control, she saw a considerable mass of leaves, tree branches and similar debris moving down the stream to greet her. Deciding that getting into an argument with the debris was not really necessary and that she really was quite wet and that this meet was not really important, Debbie elected to retire from the course, a rare DNF for her. (Only five participants didn't finish.)

The story of the day, however, involved a storm drain system that failed. In the southeastern corner of the map a pair of 48 inch corrugated steel culverts bring the surface runoff from the surrounding residential area into Pipers Creek. Sometime in the mid morning, the culverts, clearly carrying more water than they were designed for, failed due to the water backing up inside them. That is, they began to leak and the leaking water began to erode the hillside.

Now this was no small matter. The culverts issued from a large concrete junction box about 35 meters above the creek bed. From the box, the culverts went down a 45 degree slope into the creek. Originally they were buried, but the erosion slowly exposed them. (The soil was mostly glacial sands and silts, which are readily eroded by running water. Solid bedrock is several hundred meters below the surface.) In time the flow of water removed all of the soil from around the pipes themselves and, one by one, the sections of the culverts began to break loose, falling into the ever growing chasm.

By noon, the resulting hole was large enough to cause concern to the local residents, whose houses were in clear danger of joining the used culvert in the bottom of the hole. One family even began to move out! By 2:30, when Don and I went out to check out the situation, the concrete box was sitting on top of a brand spanking new 30 meter cliff and a very impressive waterfall was issuing from it. Looking down into the hole, Don remarked "That sure wasn't like that when I put out the markers." While we watched, a couple more sections of the culvert fell off, as did a fair amount of the cliff.

What had been an unassumming hillside with a pipeline right of way down it (marked on the map in yellow) had become a 'dangerous cliff' complete with waterfall and a dozen or more 'manmade objects' (the culvert parts). No way around it - we will have to re-fieldcheck the map.

Advanced course control number six was near by as was one of the crossing points. A good route used by many on the course was to cross Pipers Creek (from the trail on the north side) and then go diagonally up to number six. In the morning this was a ordinary route. By noon, the same route was decidedly foolhardy, involving hopping across the slippery culverts underneath a threatening waterfall and an unstable cliff. Fortunately no one protested - a changing map isn't covered in the USOF rules!

As for the rest of the meet, all went well. No one got hypothermia, there was some good competition on the high school league course, and everyone seemed to enjoy themselves. As he crossed the finish line, Mike Tharp, of the Nisqually Orienteers, sought out Don and with a smile said to him: "I've got two things to say - I've never had so much fun orienteering in my life.

Epilogue: The damage to the storm drains has been estimated to be on the order of one million dollars. The repair work included building an access road alongside the creek to the base of the new waterfall/cliff. We have have not made an estimate of the fieldwork required to update the map, nor has anyone volunteered to do it...

Page 9

Orienteering Club Planning Committee Proceedings

The planning committee, comprised of Roger Sharp, Valerie Enger, Eileen Williams and Robert Miller, met on Friday, January 31.

The committee evolved the following proforma goals and guidelines for itself:

conduct orienteering brainstorming sessions conduct orienteering club planning sessions
draft recommendations to the Board of Directors focus on projects that
are specific, use existing resources, and
produce large results with little effort record planning committee proceedings
make records available to the club membership meet on a needs basis
* rotate planning committee members

The post-Carkeek Park O-meet brainstorming session notes were discussed and distilled. The responses to the question, "Why do we Orienteer?," were analyzed. The following themes seemed to emerge:

Social Exercise
Outdoors Challenge
Fun Simple/Elegant/Accessible
* Maps

The committee agreed these themes may be useful as possible goals for the Membership and the Board to use as a way of setting priorities.

The responses to the brainstorming question, "What do you want the club to do next?," were analyzed. The following activity clusters emerged:

Operations Meets
Equipment Development
Maps Publicity

When these activities were prioritized on the basis of the planning committee guidelines, STREAMLINING emerged as the most salient activity within the first cluster, Operations.

Club Dues

MEMBERSHIP COORDINATOR = CAR POOL COORDINATOR?? WHY NOT?

Since there are many club members who might go to more meets if they could ride with some one else, I feel that as membership coordinator with the most current membership roster available, I can also handle arranging car pools for getting to meets. Any who have space in their vehicles, let me know and I'll try to keep a list so that those needing rides can be matched with someone living fairly close, preferably within the same ZIP code area. I suggest $2 as a reasonable gas contribution though naturally if the car pools are to go to Canada, California, etc. then a fairer share should apply. So, any one who needs a ride to a meet call Harvey at 784-2774. For best results, call a week or so before you need the ride.

  • Harvey Friedman

To the Editor,

This is a letter of thanks to Mike Schuh and the people from COC who helped with the WIOL. I think we had 17 kids who competed at least once this winter from our school. Even though they are not all great orienteers, they all enjoyed it very much. Our kids were impressed with the competitors from the other schools (and from Canada) and salute especially the Yelm Tornados. (Tornadoes?)

And I still don't know how Mike scrounged up all that extra stuff he gave out to the top kids after the meet. (Posters, Competiton maps from Austrailia, etc) Thanks also go to Debbie and Arnold for the neat trophy.

Paul Moore
Wilson High

The Kelsey Creek Park meet on Saturday, January 11th, provided a good example of a challenging course in a small park map on which many of the participants had run before. Kudos to course designer, Bob Rein, and his coach, Dave Enger, for a course where 1- the markers were placed at eye level and on the indicated side of the feature; and 2- the time advantage went to the cross-country route choice, which encouraged orienteers to forsake the trails. The scale of the Kelsey Creek map, 1 to 3,000, being smaller than most of our park maps, gave some runners (Debbie and Robert, to mention a couple of names) the opportunity to do some mapping of their own when they ran off the map in search of controls 7 and 8 on the advanced course. P.S. - thanks to the Sammamish Club for the six new 'O' signs donated to Cascade.

Sunday, March 9th - Kelsey Creek Park, Bellevue A "motala" course will be set for the intermediate and advanced orienteers. This will involve memory, line, and score orienteering - all on the same course! The park is located on SE 4th Street just east of 128th Ave SE.

Sunday, April 6th - Island Crest Park, Mercer Island Only the second event on this new 1:7,500 scale 5-color map. If you didn't attend the last one due to the rain, don't miss this one! The area is more challenging than many of the other city parks. Located on Island Crest Way about two miles south of I-90.
Wednesday, May 14th, 5:30-7:00pm, Marymoor Park, Redmond First evening meet of the year! Meet in the picnic area east of the mansion.

Mike Schuh will be hosting a party in his apartment (1706 NW 57th #3) on Saturday evening, March 15, starting at about 7:30pm. Besides his dynamic presence, the main attraction of the evening will be a slide show of his trip to Hong Kong, and the 1986 Asia-Pacific Orienteering Championships. Please RSVP (work 356-6241, home 783-3960) and bring some munchies or something to drink.

Mike Schuh will hold a short clinic on course setting on the last two Sundays in March (23rd and 30th). Both meetings will be at his apartment (1706 NW 57th #3) from 7:00 pm to 9:00pm and will cover such topics as the basic principles of course setting, differences between novice and advanced courses, how to keep the participants happy, mistakes to avoid, how to estimate winning times, the mechanics of course printing, how to use IOF control description symbols, how to tie markers on to trees so that they can be untied yet won't blow away, and anything else he thinks of between now and then. If you are interested in the clinic, please call Mike (work 356-6241, home 783-3960) and plan to attend. The clinic will be suitable for both those who have never set courses as well as those who have.

"All of the fountains of the great abyss burst forth, and the floodgates of the sky were opened. For forty days and forty nights heavy rain poured down on the earth... Higher and higher above the earth rose the waters, until all the highest mountains everywhere were submerged..." [Genesis 7:11-12,19]
I looked out into the rainy pre-dawn gloom. It was incredibly wet and getting wetter. "If I see a line of animals walking by in pairs, I'll cancel the meet."

Don Denbo, the course setter for the day's meet, looked up from the pile of control markers he was sorting and agreed that that would be a proper course of action, especially if a large boat were to pull up to the nearby beach and begin loading the animals.

We were preparing to set out the last couple dozen markers for Cascade OC's local meet of Saturday, January 18. It was the third straight day of heavy rain in the Seattle area, heavier than usual, and we were questioning our sanity. Before the day would end, the rainfall would come close to setting a local record for 24 hour precipitation total - 4.14 inches. Thursday evening's weather had included thunderstorms with winds over 70 knots, and now it was still pouring. On Friday morning I had wondered what we would do if the bad weather continue through Saturday. On Saturday morning, faced with finding out first hand, I also wondered if anyone would show up.

The meet was in Seattle's Carkeek Park, in the northwestern part of the city along the shore of Puget Sound. In local orienteering circles, the park is infamous for its mud and steep slopes. During the spring and summer, the forest is quite thick with stinging nettles and general foliage. Aside from the weather, winter is the best time to orienteer in most of our city park maps, at least as far as visibility is concerned. But, as might be expected, winter meets run the risk of inclement winter weather, heavy rains being the most common. The period of January 16-18, however, was exceptionally wet.

By 9:00 we had all of the control markers in place and I began to wonder if the rest of the meet staff would show up (our local meets begin at 10:00). One of the first cars to arrive, however, was a gentleman and his daughter out to try orienteering for their first time. A quick question confirmed my suspicion: like myself, they were natives of the Seattle area - a little bit of rain was not going to keep THEM indoors!

By 10:00 nearly all of my meet staff had arrived along with a surprisingly good turnout - a total of fifty-five individuals, plus the small handful of meet workers. Somehow we did not have enough control cards, and so everyone was obliged to punch along the edges of their maps. It turned out that this problem, and the corresponding complication of results processing, was the worst problem that we had all day. That and the fact that, for the first time in my experience, the map actually changed during the course of the event.

Now, stories of heavy rains and blizzards are plentiful in the growing folklore of American orienteering. There is the story of the [1984? 1982? - forget which year] Troll cup being buried under snow and subsequently rescheduled. Or the 1983 Missouri State Championships, where the rains turned "crossable streams" into "impassable rivers". But I have a story that might top even those legends.

Aware of the unfairness that rising stormwaters can introduce in an orienteering course, Don marked on a map corrections map and the master maps mandatory (and safe) crossing points. This worked well. But we began to get reports from some of the early finishers that not all of the 'crossing points'

were still crossable. Clearly, Pipers Creek, which runs the length of the map, was rising. Some of the tributaries were also rising, as Debbie Newell found out when she approached the seventh control on the advanced course.

As she considered going up the side stream that lead to the control, she saw a considerable mass of leaves, tree branches and similar debris moving down the stream to greet her. Deciding that getting into an argument with the debris was not really necessary and that she really was quite wet and that this meet was not really important, Debbie elected to retire from the course, a rare DNF for her. (Only five participants didn't finish.)

The story of the day, however, involved a storm drain system that failed. In the southeastern corner of the map a pair of 48 inch corrugated steel culverts bring the surface runoff from the surrounding residential area into Pipers Creek. Sometime in the mid morning, the culverts, clearly carrying more water than they were designed for, failed due to the water backing up inside them. That is, they began to leak and the leaking water began to erode the hillside.

Now this was no small matter. The culverts issued from a large concrete junction box about 35 meters above the creek bed. From the box, the culverts went down a 45 degree slope into the creek. Originally they were buried, but the erosion slowly exposed them. (The soil was mostly glacial sands and silts, which are readily eroded by running water. Solid bedrock is several hundred meters below the surface.) In time the flow of water removed all of the soil from around the pipes themselves and, one by one, the sections of the culverts began to break loose, falling into the ever growing chasm.

By noon, the resulting hole was large enough to cause concern to the local residents, whose houses were in clear danger of joining the used culvert in the bottom of the hole. One family even began to move out! By 2:30, when Don and I went out to check out the situation, the concrete box was sitting on top of a brand spanking new 30 meter cliff and a very impressive waterfall was issuing from it. Looking down into the hole, Don remarked "That sure wasn't like that when I put out the markers." While we watched, a couple more sections of the culvert fell off, as did a fair amount of the cliff.

What had been an unassumming hillside with a pipeline right of way down it (marked on the map in yellow) had become a 'dangerous cliff' complete with waterfall and a dozen or more 'manmade objects' (the culvert parts). No way around it - we will have to re-fieldcheck the map.

Advanced course control number six was near by as was one of the crossing points. A good route used by many on the course was to cross Pipers Creek (from the trail on the north side) and then go diagonally up to number six. In the morning this was a ordinary route. By noon, the same route was decidedly foolhardy, involving hopping across the slippery culverts underneath a threatening waterfall and an unstable cliff. Fortunately no one protested - a changing map isn't covered in the USOF rules!

As for the rest of the meet, all went well. No one got hypothermia, there was some good competition on the high school league course, and everyone seemed to enjoy themselves. As he crossed the finish line, Mike Tharp, of the Nisqually Orienteers, sought out Don and with a smile said to him: "I've got two things to say - I've never had so much fun orienteering in my life.

Epilogue: The damage to the storm drains has been estimated to be on the order of one million dollars. The repair work included building an access road alongside the creek to the base of the new waterfall/cliff. We have have not made an estimate of the fieldwork required to update the map, nor has anyone volunteered to do it...

When these activities were prioritized on the basis of the planning committee guidelines, STREAMLINING emerged as the most salient activity within the first cluster, Operations.

MEMBERSHIP COORDINATOR = CAR POOL COORDINATOR?? WHY NOT?

Since there are many club members who might go to more meets if they could ride with some one else, I feel that as membership coordinator with the most current membership roster available, I can also handle arranging car pools for getting to meets. Any who have space in their vehicles, let me know and I'll try to keep a list so that those needing rides can be matched with someone living fairly close, preferably within the same ZIP code area. I suggest $2 as a reasonable gas contribution though naturally if the car pools are to go to Canada, California, etc. then a fairer share should apply. So, any one who needs a ride to a meet call Harvey at 784-2774. For best results, call a week or so before you need the ride.

This is a letter of thanks to Mike Schuh and the people from COC who helped with the WIOL. I think we had 17 kids who competed at least once this winter from our school. Even though they are not all great orienteers, they all enjoyed it very much. Our kids were impressed with the competitors from the other schools (and from Canada) and salute especially the Yelm Tornados. (Tornadoes?)

And I still don't know how Mike scrounged up all that extra stuff he gave out to the top kids after the meet. (Posters, Competiton maps from Austrailia, etc) Thanks also go to Debbie and Arnold for the neat trophy.
Cascade Orienteering Club PO Box 31375 Seattle, WA 98103
